    message.setProperty("Weather.SunriseLocal",    sunriseLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.SunsetLocal",     sunsetLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.LocalDate",       localDate)
    message.setProperty("Weather.CategoryColor",   color)
    
    return message
}
  • Click Apply

STEPS 7: Router — Normal vs Default

To evaluate weather severity and decide whether to send an email alert or stop the flow.

  • Drag the Router into the flow.Place it after Groovy

  • Create Two Branches 
    • Branch 1 — Normal Weather (No Email)
      • Click on the first branch line.
      • Add the condition:
      • Expression Type: Non-XML
        • Condition: ${property.Weather.Category} = 'Normal'
        • Connect this branch directly to the End Event. This stops the flow and no email is sent.
    • Branch 2 — Default Branch (Send Email)
      • Click on the second branch.
      • Mark it as Default Route.
      • This path should connect to your End Message → Mail Sender.

This ensures that we only send alerts when the weather crosses defined thresholds.

STEPS 7: Mail Receiver

  • Drag a Receiver to the canvas and connect it to the End Message. 
  • Rename it to Mail. 
    (This name will help you identify that it is used for the weather API call.) 
  • Draw a connector from the End Message step to Mail. 
  • When prompted for adapter selection, choose MAIL Adapter.

Mail Receiver — HTML Email with Modern UI 

  • Connection Tab
    • Address: smtp.gmail.com:587 
    • Authentication: Plain User/Password 
    • Credential Name: Enter the User Credential you created earlier in the Security Material.

  • Processing Tab
    • From: Your Gmail ID (the same email used to generate the App Password) 
    • To: Receiver email address 
    • Subject: 
Weather • ${property.Weather.Location}: ${property.Weather.TempC}°C (${property.Weather.Category}) • ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset} 
    • Body: Paste your HTML + CSS email UI Code inside the Body section. 
    • Body Mime Type: Text/HTML

HTML/CSS Code
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>Weather Update – ${property.Weather.Location}</title>
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"/>
<style>
  body { font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; margin:0; padding:0; color:#1f2937; }
  .container { max-width: 640px; margin: 24px auto; background: #ffffff; border-radius: 16px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 10px 25px rgba(17,24,39,.08); }
  .header { background: #2563eb; color:#fff; padding: 20px 24px; }
  .title { margin:0; font-size: 20px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .2px; }
  .subtitle { margin:6px 0 0; font-size: 13px; opacity: .95; }
  .content { padding: 20px 24px; }
  .tempCard { display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:space-between; background:#f8fafc; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:16px; margin:8px; }
  .tempMain { font-size: 38px; font-weight: 800; color:#111827; }
  .tempFeel { font-size: 14px; color:#6b7280; }
  .badge { display:inline-block; padding:6px 12px; font-size:12px; border-radius:999px; background:${property.Weather.CategoryColor}; color:#fff; }
  .row { display:flex; gap:20px; flex-wrap: wrap; }
  .card { flex:1 1 180px; min-width:180px; background:#ffffff; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:14px; margin:8px;}
  .card h4 { margin:0 0 8px; font-size:13px; color:#374151; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.5px; }
  .value { font-size:16px; color:#111827; font-weight:600; }
  .dim { color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
  .footer { padding: 16px 24px; background:#f8fafc; border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb; color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
</style>
</head>

<body>
  <div class="container">
    
    <div class="header">
      <h1 class="title">Weather in ${property.Weather.Location}</h1>
      <p class="subtitle">
        As of ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} (UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset})
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="content">
      
      <div class="tempCard">
        <div>
          <div class="tempMain">${property.Weather.TempC}°C</div>
          <div class="tempFeel">
            Feels like ${property.Weather.FeelsLikeC}°C • 
            ${property.Weather.Condition} – ${property.Weather.Description}
          </div>
        </div>
        <div>
          <span class="badge">${property.Weather.Category}</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="row">

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Humidity</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Humidity}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Comfort varies with temp & wind</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Pressure</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Pressure} hPa</div>
          <div class="dim">Sea-level equivalent</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Visibility</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.VisibilityKm} km</div>
          <div class="dim">Reported by station</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Wind</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.WindSpeedMS} m/s • 
            ${property.Weather.WindDir} (${property.Weather.WindDeg}°)
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Direction & speed</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Cloud Cover</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.CloudsPct}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Fraction of sky covered</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Sunrise / Sunset</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.SunriseLocal} • ${property.Weather.SunsetLocal}
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Local times</div>
        </div>

      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="footer">
      Data source: OpenWeather • 
      Local date: ${property.Weather.LocalDate} • 
      Timezone: UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset}
    </div>

  </div>
</body>
</html>

DEPLOY & TESTING

STEPS 1: Deploy the iFlow

  • Click Deploy in CPI. 
  • Wait until the deployment status becomes “Started”. 
  • Go to Monitor → Manage Integration Content. 
  • Confirm the iFlow is Started (green status).

STEPS 2: Test the iFlow 

  • Set the schedule to a short interval (e.g., every 5 minutes) during testing. 
  • Once the scheduled time arrives, CPI will trigger the iFlow automatically.
  • Go to Monitor → Message Processing and verify: 
  • Status: Completed 
  • Check your email inbox for the weather alert.

CONCLUSION

With just a few essential steps—Timer, API call via Request‑Reply, Groovy-based temperature evaluation, and the Mail adapter—you can deliver clear and modern HTML weather alerts exactly when they are needed. This approach can also be adapted for other rule‑based notification scenarios such as air‑quality updates, inventory thresholds, or SLA exception alerts.

If you’d like to extend this further, you can experiment with features like multi‑city summaries, analytical dashboards, or sending notifications to channels like Microsoft Teams or Slack.

If you have any questions or would like to share your feedback, feel free to drop a comment!

entry.title: Time zone conversion for message mapping in Integration Suite
entry.content:
Time zone conversion is not often used in Integration Suite, as date and time are mostly converted to different time zone in target system for most integrations. More recently however, with more standardized and cloud systems being used (e.g S4HANA Cloud), as well as prepackaged API's, time zone conversion has become more common. In addition, most countries use daylight savings time, which requires a different time offset depending on the day and month for date and time.

For SAP PO, the most common way to convert date and time to a different timezone is to use java libraries for Joda-Time in a user defined function to convert date and time to a different timezone. For Integration Suite, these libraries can be used by default in groovyscript. Following an earlier post with an example of how to use these libraries in groovy, as well as some changes to the code to make the function more generic, the code can be used in message mapping to convert date and time to a different timezone.

import java.util.TimeZone;
import org.joda.time.DateTime;
import org.joda.time.DateTimeZone;
import org.joda.time.format.DateTimeFormat;
import org.joda.time.format.DateTimeFormatter;

def String converttimezone(String input, String pattern, String totimezone) {
    
    //set output to empty
    def output = "";

    //if input is not empty, convert input to timezone
    if (input != "") {
        DateTimeFormatter dtformatter = DateTimeFormat.forPattern(pattern);
        DateTime datetime = dtformatter.parseDateTime(input);
        TimeZone timezone = TimeZone.getTimeZone(totimezone);
        output = datetime.withZone(DateTimeZone.forTimeZone(timezone)).toString();
    }
    
    return output;
}

The function uses parameters input, pattern and totimezone to convert a date and time to a different timezone

  • input: date and time with offset or timezone (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00)
  • pattern: format for date and time, using notation for SimpleDateFormat

    Most commonly used notation:

    • y - year
    • M - month
    • d - day
    • H - hour
    • m - minutes
    • s - seconds
    • S - milliseconds
    • Z - time offset (e.g +01:00, +0100)
    • ZZZ - time zone (e.g UTC, Europe/Amsterdam); this can be any identifier for time zone
      • Any text which is not part of the date and time notation can be enclosed in apostrophe (e.g yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss)
  • totimezone: any identifier for time zone (e.g UTC, Europe/Amsterdam)

The output date and time format for the function is always the same, either yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00Z) when converting date and time to UTC, or yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZ (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00) for any other time zone. If needed, the standard datetrans function can be used in message mapping to change the date and time to a different format.

Examples

Example 1: convert date and time with offset (e.g 2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00) to UTC

In this case date and time with offset is used from source message and converted to UTC timezone

  • input: date and time with offset (2026-03-26T18:00:00+01:00)
  • pattern: date and time with offset (yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ssZ)
  • totimezone: UTC

After converting date and time to UTC, the format is changed to remove milliseconds using datetrans function (format yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' to yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss)

Example 2: convert date and time without offset from timezone CET to timezone UTC

In this case date and time is used without offset and timezone has to be added before converting date and time to different timezone

  • input: date and time, timezone is CET and is added using concat function (e.g 2026-02-25 17:59:23 CET)
  • pattern: date and time with offset (yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss ZZZ)
  • totimezone: timezone is UTC

After converting date and time to UTC, format is changed back to original using datetrans function (format yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSS'Z' to yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss)

Example 3: convert date and time from timezone UTC to timezone Europe/Amsterdam

  • input: date and time, timezone is added (e.g 2026-08-27T12:00:00 UTC)
  • pattern: date and time with timezone (yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss ZZZ)
  • totimezone: date and time is set to timezone Europe/Amsterdam

Because offset can be different depending on the day and month for daylight savings time, milliseconds and offset are removed using substring function.

 

References:

  • Timezone conversion for SAP PO: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/time-zone-conversion-in-pi-mapping/ba-p/13156873
  • Earlier example of function to convert date and time to different timezone: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45834774/groovy-date-timezone-formatting
  • Full list of class SimpleDateFormat, used for date and time notation: http://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/java/text/SimpleDateFormat.html
  • List of all timezones: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones

entry.title: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration - Alert Rules via SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Alert Rules via SAP Cloud ALM

Objective

The goal of this blog is to explain how to migrate your alert rules from SAP Process Orchestration and set up proactive monitoring for SAP Integration Suite exclusively using SAP Cloud ALM. We will cover the end-to-end setup including:

  • SAP BTP Cockpit configuration and endpoint creation.
  • Service and Endpoint configuration within SAP Cloud ALM.
  • Setting up alerts, filters, and event actions in Integration & Exception Monitoring.

SAP PO Alerts
In SAP Process Orchestration, you are likely used to creating Alert Rules in the NetWeaver Administrator (NWA) or Integration Directory. This is where you managed your active rules and their consumers (like ALERT-TO-MAIL).

As you can see below in the Message Header Rules tab, we would specifically filter our alerts using Integration Directory objects, such as the Service Interface, Communication Component, or the full Integrated Configuration (ICO):

Understanding the Transition to SAP Cloud ALM

When migrating your integration scenarios from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite, recreating your alerting mechanisms is a critical step. By using SAP Cloud ALM for your monitoring, you establish a connection point that improves visibility for all connected systems and ensures that integration errors are automatically detected and communicated.

Step 1: SAP BTP Cockpit Configuration

To connect SAP Cloud ALM with SAP Integration Suite, you must first create an endpoint in the SAP BTP Cockpit capable of retrieving Message Logs and Artifact Health data.

  • Navigate to your Subaccount from the Global Account in SAP BTP Cockpit.
  • Go to Services -> Instances and Subscriptions and choose Create.
  • Select the Process Integration Runtime Service and choose the integration-flow plan.

  • Add the following roles to the instance: MonitoringDataRead and MonitoringArtifactsDeploy.

  • After creating the instance, open it to create a Service Key.
  • Provide a descriptive name, set the Key Type to Client ID/Secret, and save the key in JSON format securely, as it will be required in later steps.

Step 2: SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

With the BTP endpoint prepared, the next phase is establishing the connection in SAP Cloud ALM. The user performing this needs specific roles: x_landscape_Administrator and x_landscape_SecurityAdministrator.

  • Access Administration and navigate to Landscape Management.

  • Under Services & Systems, you can attempt to automatically import your service by navigating to Configuration -> Import of Subscribed SAP Services.

  • If an automatic import isn't possible (which typically happens if the daily synchronization job hasn't run yet, the service is registered under a different customer number, or local access controls restrict your visibility), you can manually add a New Service. To do this, define parameters such as the Name and the System Number. To find your System Number, log into SAP for Me, navigate to 'Portfolio & Products' > 'My Product Portfolio', click on your specific product, and look under the 'Systems' tab for the tenant's unique identifier. Finally, select the Service Type (SAP Integration Suite) and input the Root URL / Logon URL that you copied from your saved Service Key.

  • Once the service is created, select Add in the Endpoints section and paste the Service Key details you saved earlier to automatically fill the parameters, then click Check Connection.

Step 3: Setting Up Alerts and Notifications

Now that the external systems are connected, you can configure the actual alert rules using the Integration & Exception Monitoring section in Operations.

To perform this, users need roles such as ops_im_IntegrationArchitect, ops_im_IntegrationOwner, or x_calm_GlobalAdministrator.

  • Select your newly configured service from the Managed Components list (under Services) and click Apply.

  • Access Edit Configuration for the service and turn on Data Collection to begin gathering monitoring data

 

  • Navigate to Events to add the specific events used for alerts.

  • For individual Integration Flow exceptions, add an event and set the Event Name to "Erroneous CPI Message Detected (Single)". To monitor specific messages or restrict events to certain integration flows, you can create custom filters. In the Filters tab, you can define filter parameters for specific integration flows. For example, you can limit the alert to a single interface by specifying its Artifact Name or Artifact ID, or include multiple interfaces by providing a list of names. Furthermore, you can use powerful combinations by pairing the Artifact Name with specific Statuses or Error Messages (e.g., only triggering the alert if 'iFlow_A' fails with an HTTP 500 error).  For those of used to SAP PO, think of the Artifact Name filter as the direct replacement for the Service Interface or ICO filters we saw in the Message Header Rules at the beginning of this post.

  • You can define specific parameters (like triggering the event for errors older than 5 minutes) and use the Filters tab to restrict the events to specific integration flows.

Post-Configuration Activities: Event Actions and Customizing Output

Just like manual fine-tuning steps in a interface migration, your event routing will need a few final touches:

 

  • Select verified recipients from the Recipient section. If your recipients aren't listed, add them by going to SAP Cloud ALM > Operations > Notification Management, clicking the '+' icon, and entering the target email addresses separated by commas.

 

  • When an error matches your defined parameters and filters, the system will automatically trigger an email notification containing key details like the integration artifact, message ID, status, and timestamp.

  • For single erroneous messages, you can highly customize the information displayed in the email up to 7 fields by choosing the appropriate attributes on the Grouping tab during Event configuration.

Conclusion

Migrating your alert rules to SAP Cloud ALM simplifies the monitoring process while guaranteeing that your SAP Integration Suite landscape remains highly visible. By establishing a secure BTP endpoint and defining granular event filters and notification actions, you can ensure reliable, scalable, and proactive error handling for all your newly migrated integration interfaces.

For additional questions related to the SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite migration, see FAQ – Migration from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite automatic deployments using Ansible
entry.content:
Introduction 

Ansible was included in the architecture with the sole purpose of automating repetitive deployment tasks, reducing the effort, the time spent, restricting the roles/accesses in the the productive tenant, and most important, human errors. By creating only once a file with instructions, Ansible will be able to go through each SAP environment, dynamically execute the steps mentioned, configure parameters, create credentials, deploy artifacts, etc. 

When transporting artifact changes from DEV up to PROD, we, as developers, might need to configure manually, for each system, some of the following: 

  • Users Credential Creation  
  • Updating Known Hosts 
  • Service Keys in SAP Business Technology Platform 
  • Updating external parameters  
  • Import certificates 
  • Deploying integration artifacts  
  • Adding JDBC Material 
  • Configuring Trading Partner Management / API Management 
  • Other 

By using this automated approach, the developers will set up a list of instructions only once, and they will be able to execute these steps as many times as needed.  

Architecture Overview 

Picture 1 - Architecture Overview

 

ServiceNow and Github as source of truth 

For the sake of observability and audit, we wanted to have as much control as possible and be able to see who did what, when, and how. 

With this in mind, we decided that ServiceNow Change Requests together with Github, will be our sources of truth. 

 

  1. Each ServiceNow CHG will contain the Ansible instructions file. 
  1. Each ServiceNow CHG will contain the Jenkins build log. 
  1. Github will hold all the generated Ansible playbooks, failed and successful instructions. 
  1. Github will hold all the sensitive data ( certificates, credentials, etc. ), encrypted using ansible-vault. 

How it works?  

Ansible will start right after the transport stage is done, so the latest artifact version is present in the target environment. 

It will look for a special instructions file in ServiceNow CHGs, and in case it finds it, it starts the process. This instruction file is YAML format, something that can be easily understood by humans, and at the same time by programs. Ansible goes through all steps mentioned in the file and executes them one by one. 

Picture 2 - Sample Ansible Task

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Each step has a list of parameters and a reference to something called a 'task'.  

For us, tasks are API calls, like we do in Postman – set the url, set the body, headers, authentication, etc. 

For Ansible, tasks are logic blocks which contain full details about the API call, can receive a list of parameters to iterate over, can validate response codes, can extract values from the response body, etc. 

Wait. We don't use the same parameters in all environments. 

Sometimes we do, sometimes we don't. For scenarios where the parameters are the same, you can just import and start the pipeline, and the job is done. 

For scenarios where the parameters are different (the usual cases):  

  • Import the yaml, where the whole list of steps is ALREADY defined 
  • Modify the different parameters (Change '100' to '200' for sap-client f.e. ) 
  • Start the pipeline 
  • Done 

Ansible will take care of the actions done on the SAP Integration Suite tenants, it will create security materials, it will save everything in Github, it will log everything in Jenkins, it will validate what you filled in the steps. No more typos, no more errors, no more forgetting to add a certificate, a ssh key, forgot to deploy, etc. 

I am a developer, how do I use it? 

The first step would be to go into the template generator page and check all the needed configuration steps. 

Picture 3 - Template Page

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After all steps are configured as per the needs of the ServiceNow Change Request, the developer will just review the generated template and push it into the  

Change Request. The template will be automatically pushed into the ServiceNow Change Request, as an attachment, consolidating traceability. 

Picture 4 - Template preview

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After that, using Jenkins, he can trigger a pipeline to start Ansible and view the logs.  

Picture 5 - SNOW Attachments

 

 

 

 

 

 

Below is a sample Jenkins build log, in which you will see how Ansible is triggered to get the last modified date of a package and create a service key in our SAP Business Technology Platform subaccount.

[Pipeline] echo 
EXECUTING ANSIBLE PLAYBOOK 
[Pipeline] echo 
Playbook: implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
[Pipeline] echo 
Environment: preprod 
[Pipeline] echo 
Ansible Host: localhost:2345 
[Pipeline] echo 
Triggered By: [Admin] 
[Pipeline] echo 
Start Time: 2026-02-12 14:10:24 
[Pipeline] echo 
============================================================ 
[Pipeline] httpRequest 
HttpMethod: POST 
URL: http://localhost:2345/run-playbook/implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Content-Type: application/json 
Sending request to url: http://localhost:2345/run-playbook/implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Response Code: HTTP/1.1 200 OK 
Response:  
[WARNING]: Found variable using reserved name: environment 
 
PLAY [Build #22 -- Playbook for CHG047545_preprod] ***************************** 
 
TASK [Task 0 - Initialize global env variable] ********************************* 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [DEBUG - Check environment value] ***************************************** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "The value of environment is 'preprod' and global_env_variable is 'preprod'" 
} 
 
TASK [Task 1 - Initialize global aggregator] *********************************** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [Task 2 - Execute Authentication] ***************************************** 
 
TASK [0.1 - Get OAuth2 Access Token] ******************************************* 
 
TASK [tools : Step 1 - POST Call for fetching OAuth2 Access Token.] ************ 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Step 2 - Extracting access_token from the json response.] ******** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [0.2 - Get CPI API X-CSRF-Token] ****************************************** 
 
TASK [tools : Step 1 - GET Call for fetching X-CSRF-Token.] ******************** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Step 2 - Extracting X-CSRF-Token from json response.] ************ 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [auth_helper : 0.3 - Debug current tokens] ******************************** 
skipping: [localhost] 
 
TASK [Task 3 - Execute get-package-last-modified] ****************************** 
 
TASK [integration-flow : Task 1 - Get Integration Package by ID and return the integration package] *** 
ok: [localhost] => (item=SAPCPIPackage1) 
 
TASK [integration-flow : Task 2 - Extract response from x.msg.results[0].json.d] *** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [integration-flow : Task 3 - Display the last modified date (dd/mm/yyyy HH:MM:SS)] *** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "Last modified date: 02/02/2026 18:15:12" 
} 
 
TASK [Task 4 - Execute get_service_instance_by_name] *************************** 
 
TASK [Task 1 - Get CF OAuth Token] ********************************************* 
 
TASK [tools : Task 1 - Get CF OAuth Token] ************************************* 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Task 2 - Print OK if token was retrieved successfully] *********** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "OAuth token retrieval: OK" 
} 
 
TASK [tools : Task 3 - Print NOT OK if token retrieval failed] ***************** 
skipping: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Task 4 - Extract access_token from cf_oauth_token_response] ****** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 2 - Get Service Instance by Name] ************************** 
ok: [localhost] => (item=None) 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 3 - Return message ok if status code is 200 or 202] ******** 
ok: [localhost] => (item=SK-ProcessIntegrationRuntime) => { 
    "msg": "Service Instance found successfully for SK-ProcessIntegrationRuntime" 
} 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 4 - Return message error if status code is not 200 or 202] *** 
skipping: [localhost] => (item=SK-ProcessIntegrationRuntime)  
skipping: [localhost] 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 5 - Extract resources.guid from service_instance] ********** 
ok: [localhost] => (item=None) 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [Task 5 - Execute create_service_key] ************************************* 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 0 - Build service key body] ********************************

TASK [Task 1 - Get CF OAuth Token] ********************************************* 
 
TASK [tools : Task 1 - Get CF OAuth Token] ************************************* 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Task 2 - Print OK if token was retrieved successfully] *********** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "OAuth token retrieval: OK" 
} 
 
TASK [tools : Task 3 - Print NOT OK if token retrieval failed] ***************** 
skipping: [localhost] 
 
TASK [tools : Task 4 - Extract access_token from cf_oauth_token_response] ****** 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 2 - Create Service Key] ************************************ 
ok: [localhost] 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 3 - Return message ok if status code is 200 or 202] ******** 
ok: [localhost] => { 
    "msg": "Service Key created successfully" 
} 
 
TASK [cf-api : Task 4 - Return message error if status code is not 200 or 202] *** 
skipping: [localhost] 
 
PLAY RECAP ********************************************************************* 
localhost                  : ok=22   changed=0    unreachable=0    failed=0    skipped=5    rescued=0    ignored=0    
 
 
============================================================ 
EXECUTION SUMMARY: 
Playbook: implementation_steps_CHG047545_preprod.yml 
Environment: preprod 
Duration: 7343ms 
Exit Code: 0 
Status: SUCCESS 
============================================================ 
 
Success code from [200‥200] 
[Pipeline] echo 
 
============================================================ 
[Pipeline] echo 
EXECUTION COMPLETED

Reusability 

Picture 6 - Reusability

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

If the developer wants to continue to the next environment, it can just import the previously generated template on the UI template-generator page, change the environment (replace 'test' with 'preprod' in a dropdown) and push the template again. 

Available actions 

We have converted the whole API spec of SAP Integration Suite into dynamic, modular Ansible tasks. Basically anything that is available on SAP Business Accelerator Hub, can be triggered using Ansible.  

 

Picture 7 - Available Tasks

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Security and encryption 

In the instructions file, visible in the ServiceNow attachments and Github storage, we will only see references, no plain text credentials, certificates, API tokens, etc. 

All these references are being used to extract the actual data from a CHG-specific vars file, along with the global vars credentials shared across all ServiceNow Change Request. 

These vars files are using a standard functionality of Ansible, called Ansible Vault. 

Ansible Vault relies on the following cryptographic components: 

  • Cipher: AES-256 in CTR (Counter) mode. 
  • Key Derivation: It uses PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) to turn our plain-text passwords into a high-entropy encryption key. 
  • Hashing: HMAC-SHA256.  
  • Salt: A random salt is generated for every encryption operation, ensuring that the same password and the same plain-text result in a different cipher text every time. 

For example, if we want to deploy a JDBC material  

Picture 9 - Add JDBC

 

 

 

 

 

We will use the CPISNOW_PREPROD reference in the instruction file. This address, along with its details, will be added automatically in that CHGs vars file  

Picture 10 - Vars file

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Above you can see how the vars file looks decrypted. It is a dummy JDBC server, in which all the required fields to deploy the JDBC material are filled. By using a vault pass file, Ansible will decrypt, extract and encrypt back all the files whenever they are needed. Besides that, everything is secured and maintained correctly. 

Auditing & Traceability 

Traceability

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

By saving these recipes as code, they can be subject to an automatic/manual code review process, they can be traced to whoever triggered the pipelines and they're auditable. At any given moment, we have access to the entire workflow – we can see the configured steps, we can see the parameters that were added, we can see the Git commit logs, we can see timestamps, authors, etc. 

Conclusions 

Using automated deployments, the error rate would significantly decrease, since the developer will not go manually and repeat the steps on each environment. The only place where it would be able to make any sort of mistakes would be when generating the template, but even so, the html form contains a lot of validations, and also the dropdowns are dynamically generated with actual data from the system, so it would minimize the error rate. 

Including Github and Jenkins, everything is logged, everything is tracked, everything can be reverted and, in the end, the developers would not even need edit roles in PREPROD/PROD, because every configuration will be done through the technical user. 

 

 

If you have any questions, feel free to reply here, or even go to the dedicated Q&A Section for SAP Integration Suite.

Thank you for your time !

entry.title: Currency Conversion Integration using SAP CPI with Groovy and XSLT
entry.content:
Introduction

In many real-world integration scenarios, applications need to handle data across different currencies. Whether it’s for reporting, billing, or analytics, converting currency values accurately and in real time is an important requirement.

In this blog, I’ll walk through how I built a simple and practical currency conversion integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). The iFlow accepts a JSON request, calls an external API to fetch exchange rates, processes the response using Groovy, and transforms the output into a clean, readable format using XSLT. Finally, the result is sent as an email, making it easy for end users to view.

 

Integration Flow Overview

This integration flow performs the following:

  • Accepts a currency conversion request in JSON format
  • Calls an external exchange rate API
  • Converts the source amount into target currency
  • Transforms the response into a readable HTML format
  • Sends the final output via Email

 

Integration Flow Diagram

HTTPS Sender → JSON to XML Converter → Content Modifier (Extract Properties) → Request Reply (External API Call) → Groovy Script (Processing) → Content Modifier (Build XML) → XSLT Mapping (HTML Output) → Email Receiver

Below is the iFlow implemented for this scenario:

The iFlow demonstrates message splitting, dynamic routing, and CSV transformation before sending data to respective receivers.

Input Payload Structure

Step 1: JSON to XML Conversion

The incoming JSON payload is converted into XML to enable XPath-based extraction in subsequent steps.

Step 2: Extracting Properties 

Using Content Modifier, the following fields are extracted:

 

 
 
These are stored as exchange properties for reuse.

Step 3: External API Call 

A Request Reply step is used to call the exchange rate API dynamically:

 Step 4: Groovy Script Processing

The API response is processed using Groovy to extract relevant fields and format the converted amount.

 Step 5: Building Output XML

A structured XML is created:

 Step 6: XSLT Transformation

The XML is transformed into an HTML table using XSLT to make the output readable.

 Step 7: Email Notification

 The final formatted result is sent via Email using the Mail adapter.

 

 Challenges Faced 

  • Handling API authentication issues
  • Parsing dynamic JSON responses
  • Resolving null pointer exceptions
  • Ensuring correct data formatting

 

Key Learnings

  • Importance of validating API responses
  • Effective use of Groovy for JSON processing
  • Combining XML and XSLT for formatting
  • Designing efficient CPI iFlows

 

Conclusion

This use case demonstrates how SAP CPI can be leveraged to build real-time integrations involving external APIs, data transformation, and user-friendly output delivery.

This implementation helped me better understand how to design flexible and maintainable integration flows, especially when dealing with real-world requirements.

I hope this experience helps others who are working on similar scenarios in SAP Cloud Integration.

Happy to hear feedback or suggestions from the community.

entry.title: Cloud Integration Pipeline - Datastore Extension UI
entry.content:
⚠️  Disclaimer: The UI described in this blog post is not an official product by SAP. If you are interested in using the UI, you can contact @lisarizzo or @mike_sibler.  It will soon also be available as an SAP Open Source project.  

 

Purpose and background

The pipeline in Cloud Integration is a well-established framework for asynchronous integrations. A blog post to explain the starting points for the pipeline can be found here.

In addition to the pipeline, we offer the datastore extension, which simplifies error handling via automatic retry jobs and APIs for resending and cancelling messages. For details on the datastore extension, please refer to this blog post. The datastore extension package provides automatic restart capabilities with Restart Jobs that are based on pre-delivered IFlows. In addition, it provides on-demand operations that require the use of API client tools such as Postman or Bruno. This is technical and error-prone to handle from a user perspective.

The UI introduced in this blog post is designed to provide a simplified and user-friendly approach to access these on-demand APIs.

On a technical level, it is relevant to note that the UI does not call Cloud Integration APIs directly. It calls IFlows of the Datastore Extension package, which are on-demand API Operations (Manage Retries and Manage Datastore Entries). It triggers a corresponding IFlow and operation and then displays the response.

This blog article introduces and explains the functionality of the Cloud Integration Pipeline Datastore Extension UI.

 

Overview of key features

The current version of the UI provides these capabilities, which are shown again in more detail in the “How to use the UI” section.

  • Visual overview of all available datastores relevant for the pipeline reprocessing, their messages and overdue messages
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific datastores
  • Visual overview of all messages in each datastore
  • Search and filter capabilities to quickly locate specific messages
  • Action to restart messages from the UI
  • Action to move messages to another datastore or a no-retry datastore when located in a retry datastore
  • Action to delete/cancel messages 
  • Detailed view of message content (error, headers, payload)
  • Possibility to perform batch actions to restart, move, cancel several messages at once

 

How to use the UI

Datastore overview

The first screen of the UI shows a table with all the datastores, the number of messages, and the number of overdue messages. You can also search and filter the table for specific information. 

Datastore detail view and overview of messages

The second screen shows details of the selected datastore and contains a table with all the messages. 

One or more messages can be selected in the table and moved to another datastore or to a no-retry datastore. The messages can also be restarted or deleted from here.  

There are sorting capabilities available for the datastore entries table. They can be found behind the gear icon ⚙️. For example, the table can be sorted by the number of restarts and the timestamp of error, ascending or descending. 

There is also a filter hidden behind the same gear icon ⚙️.

Message detail view

When clicking on one of the rows in the datastore entries table the user is directed to a third screen with details about the error, headers and payload. If, after inspecting, the user decided what to do with the message it is also possible to perform the same move, delete and restart actions from this screen with the buttons in the top right corner.

 

Outlook & how to implement the UI

This project will soon be available on the SAP GitHub. Until then, for information on how to implement the Datastore Extension UI, please reach out to lisa.rizzo@sap.com or mike.sibler@sap.com . Once the project is published on the SAP GitHub as an open-source project, you can find the link to the repository and a detailed implementation guide here in this blog post.

entry.title: MongoDB Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
MongoDB adapter is a Cloud Integration capability available on SAP Integration Suite that allows you to access a MongoDB database and perform CRUD operations on data stored in MongoDB. The adapter works with MongoDB's APIs, which allow developers to access and integrate its components into their applications, enabling the management of documents and collections.

MongoDB Adapter Capabilities and Features:

  • Functions as a receiver adapter that allows you to work with MongoDB Document, Collections and Databases entities.
  • Supports LDAP authentication via User Credentials.
  • Allows Advanced query operations to fetch desired data.
  • Supports Multi Update and Multi Delete capability for documents.
  • Adapter allows you to perform the Run Command operation on the Databases.

MongoDB Adapter Authentication

The MongoDB adapter supports the LDAP (PLAIN) Authentication mechanism.

The PLAIN authentication mechanism lets you authenticate to MongoDB using your LDAP username and password. It relies on the PLAIN SASL (Simple Authentication and Security Layer) protocol for LDAP-based authentication.

For more information, refer to 3.3 Application Configuration in  MongoDB Adapter User Guide.

Scenario: Insert Document to a Collection via MongoDB Adapter

In this scenario, we will upload the file using the Insert operation in the Document entity.

Connect to MongoDB

To connect the MongoDB Adapter to the MongoDB server, you need to specify the Address and select the Authentication as LDAP (PLAIN) Authentication. Specify the Credential Name as in the SAP security material. For more information about Connection tab parameters, see Connection under SAP Help.

Add Payload to Content Modifier

In the Content Modifier, the payload specifies the Employee details to the collection (Employee_2025) of the MongoDB server.

{
  "_id": "EMP001",
  "firstName": "John",
  "lastName": "Doe",
  "email": "john.doe@company.com",
  "phone": "+1-555-123-4567",
  "designation": "Software Engineer",
  "department": "IT",
  "salary": 65000,
  "dateOfJoining": "2023-05-10",
  "address": {
    "street": "123 Main Street",
    "city": "New York",
    "state": "NY",
    "zip": "10001"
  },
  "skills": ["Java", "MongoDB", "Spring Boot"],
  "status": "Active"
}

Processing Tab: Insert Document

Switch to the processing tab, select the Entity as Document and operation as Insert. Specify Database and Collection, and enable the checkbox of Enable Transaction, so that the operations are successfully applied, or if any failure occurs, none of the changes are applied.

Once you have configured the processing tab, click Save and Deploy.

Verify the Output

Once the adapter is successfully deployed in your SAP environment, an acknowledgement is returned in the response, as shown in the figure below.

Conclusion:

The MongoDB Adapter simplifies your processes and enhances upload efficiency.

For more information, see MongoDB Adapter for SAP Integration Suite.

entry.title: Sending Dynamic Slack Notifications from SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) Using Groovy Script
entry.content:
Introduction

In modern integration scenarios, real-time notifications play a crucial role in keeping teams informed about system events such as deployments, alerts, or failures.

One effective way to achieve this is by integrating SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) with collaboration tools like Slack.

In this blog, we will build an iFlow that dynamically sends Slack notifications using Groovy scripts, based on user-specific data received in a JSON payload.

Use Case / Scenario

Consider a scenario where:

  • A system sends a JSON payload containing multiple users
  • Each user needs to receive a personalized Slack notification
  • The CPI iFlow processes this payload and sends messages individually

Sample JSON Payload

iFlow Design Overview

The integration flow consists of the following steps:

  • HTTPS Sender Adapter (triggered via Postman)
  • Groovy Script (Extract common properties)
  • JSON to XML Converter
  • General Splitter (split users)
  • Groovy Script (build Slack message)
  • Open Connectors Adapter (send to Slack)

Step 1: Extract Properties Using Groovy Script

  • This script extracts common fields like priority and reference and stores them as message properties.

Step 2: JSON to XML Conversion

  • The JSON payload is converted into XML format
  • This enables XPath usage in the splitter step

Step 3: General Splitter Configuration

  • The General Splitter splits the payload into individual users
  • Each user is processed separately
  • XPath Example: /users

Step 4: Build Slack Message Using Groovy Script

  • This script dynamically constructs a Slack message for each user.

Step 5: Send Notification to Slack

Use Open Connectors Adapter

Configure Slack connector with:

  • Channel name
  • Authentication
  • Message body

Testing Using Postman

  • Send a POST request to CPI endpoint
  • Use the sample JSON payload
  • Trigger the iFlow
  • Here I used Students details as example JSON payload

Slack Output (Expected Result)

Each user will receive a message like:

 Key Benefits

  • Dynamic message generation
  • Handles multiple users efficiently
  • Real-time Slack notifications
  • Reusable Groovy scripts

Challenges Faced

During implementation, a few challenges were addressed:

  • Handling null values in payload
  • Ensuring proper JSON parsing
  • Formatting Slack messages dynamically
  • Managing splitter configuration correctly

Conclusion

Using SAP Cloud Integration with Groovy scripting, we can easily build scalable and dynamic notification systems.

This approach is highly useful for:

  • Deployment notifications
  • Alerting systems
  • Monitoring integrations

It enhances visibility and ensures teams stay updated in real time.

#SAPCPI #SAPIntegrationSuite #Groovy #SlackIntegration #Automation

 @Souravraj_Maitra

@Bibekananda1231

entry.title: Understanding Log Levels in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) provides different log levels to monitor and troubleshoot iFlows effectively. Many beginners find it confusing to understand when to use Info, Error, Debug, and Trace and how each level affects message visibility.

In this blog, I will explain each log level in a simple way along with practical usage.

What Are Log Levels in SAP CPI?

Log levels define how much information CPI captures while processing a message.

Choosing the correct log level helps you:

  • Monitor message execution
  • Troubleshoot issues efficiently
  • Avoid unnecessary performance overhead

Log Configuration in CPI

Below is the Log Configuration screen where you select the log level before running an iFlow:

 

 

 

 

 

Explanation of Each Log Level:

None         

  • No logs are recorded
  • Used for high-volume or performance-critical scenarios
  • Not recommended for troubleshooting 

Info (Default)

Captures basic events:

  • Message start
  •  Message completion
  • High-level status

Suitable for regular monitoring

Recommended for production

Error

  • Logs only when an error occurs

  • Helps identify failures without excessive logging

  • Useful in production environments

Debug    

  • Provides step-by-step execution details

  • Does NOT capture full payload

  • Helps understand flow behavior

Trace    

Captures complete details:

Payload before and after each step

  • Mapping output
  • Router conditions
  • Splitter behavior

Best for deep troubleshooting

Should be used temporarily (can impact performance)

How CPI Internally Behaves Based on Log Levels

When an iFlow runs, CPI behaves differently depending on the selected log level:

  • For Info / Error → Only summary logs are stored
  • For Debug → Step-level information is captured
  • For Trace → Full payload snapshots are stored at every step

This directly affects:

  • Message Processing Logs (MPL)
  • Trace log visibility
  • System performance

Conclusion

Log Levels are a fundamental part of SAP CPI monitoring and debugging.

Understanding when to use each level helps you:

  • Diagnose issues faster
  • Improve performance
  • Avoid unnecessary logging

Note: This blog is based on my personal learning and hands-on experience with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI).

#SAPCPI #SAPIntegration #BTP #Logging

@Bibekananda1231 

@Souravraj_Maitra

entry.title: How to Use Multiple APIs in a Single iFlow (SAP Integration Suite)
entry.content:
In real-time integration scenarios, we commonly use multiple APIs within a single integration flow (iFlow). For example, one API response is required as input for another API, or the final response may be the combination of the both API responses.

SAP Integration Suite (CPI) provides components like content modifier, adapters that helps us for multiple APIs scenarios in one iFlow.

In this blog, we will walk through a step-by-step implementation of calling two APIs within a single iFlow, capturing each response, and finally combining or processing the data before sending it back to the final response. 

Overview 

  • user triggers the iFlow using an HTTPS sender 
  • The iFlow calls API 1, captures its response
  • With in response of the API 1 it calls API 2, captures its response 
  • Finally, it processes and returns the combined responses of both APIs 

Step-by-Step Implementation 

Step 1: Create an iFlow 

Create a new Integration Flow (iFlow) in SAP Integration Suite as shown in the below figure. 

HTTPS sender --> iFlow logic (it will sequentially call the two APIs) --> HTTP receiver

 

Step 2: Configure the HTTPS Sender Adapter 

  • In the sender side give the address in the connection tab(endpoint path). It is basically the endpoint used to trigger externally (for example in our case it's postman).
  • In our iFlow I have given the address as /testweatherNote: Make sure the address starts with /<address>

 

Step 3: Configure Content Modifier 1 (API 1 Headers and Properties) 

Message Header 

Use content modifier header to externally call the API using it's host and key values.

Add the host and key value of the first API.

 Exchange Properties 

Use exchange properties to fetch the input values dynamically.

  • Name: Input tag name 
  • Source Type: XPath / Expression 
  • Source Value: XML input tag 

This property will later be used in the API call.

 Step 4: Configure Receiver 1 (HTTP Adapter – API 1) 

Based on the input, response is fetched from the external API via request reply and HTTP receiver adapter.

This request reply fetches the response from the external API and send to the next step of the flow.

  • In the Receiver 1 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 1 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step performs the actual call to the first API. 

 Step 5: Configure Content Modifier 2 (Store API 1 Response) 

Add 2nd Content Modifier to store the response of the 1st API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response1 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body} 

This stores the first API response for later use. 

 Step 6: Configure Content Modifier 3 (API 2 Headers)

Now let us call the 2nd API. As done in the earlier step we'll use the content modifier to call the 2nd API

Add the host and key value of the second API.

These headers may differ from API 1 depending on the provider. 

In Exchange property add the properties required to fetch the response from API 2.

Step 7: Configure Receiver 2 (HTTP Adapter – API 2) 

Add request reply and HTTP receiver adapter to fetch the response from the 2nd API.

  • In the Receiver 2 choose HTTP Adapter.
  • Go to Connections and Provide
    Address:  Address will be the API 2 endpoint 
    Query parameters: In query parameter to fetch the response with dynamic input use ${property.<propertyname>} expression(This allows us to dynamically read the value) .

This step triggers the second API call.

Step 8: Configure Content Modifier 4 (Store API 2 Response)  

Add 4th Content Modifier to store the response of the 2nd API.

In the exchange properties add 

  • Name: response2 
  • Source Type: Expression 
  • Source Value: ${in.body}

This stores the response of the second API. 

Step 9: Configure Content Modifier 5 (Final Message Body) 

Add content modifier 5 to store the responses of Both the APIs. This will give us the combined output of both APIs

In Content Modifier 5 Message Body Set Type: Expression and in body add

  • ${property.response1} 
  • ${property.response2} 

This step prepares the final response that combines or processes both API responses.

Step 10: Save and Deploy the iFlow 

  • Save the iFlow 
  • Deploy it successfully 

Step 11: Test Using Postman 

  • Copy the generated HTTPS endpoint 
  • Paste it into Postman 
  • Send the request 
  • Verify the combined output from both APIs  

 

Conclusion/Summary

In this blog we have covered how can we use multiple APIs in a single iFlow with help of content modifier. Also learnt about how to call external API using content modifier headers and reading the dynamic input from the postman using the content modifier exchange properties. We also saw how to take API 1 response as input for API 2 and in final result using content modifier message body we combined the both APIs responses. By this approach developers can design scalable and reusable iFlows in the real time scenarios.

entry.title: Building a Real‑Time Currency Conversion iFlow using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

While learning SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), I wanted to work on a practical, real‑time use case rather than a basic file‑based or sample integration. In many real projects, applications need to handle multiple currencies because of global customers, suppliers, and financial transactions.

To understand how CPI handles API‑based synchronous integrations, I decided to build a currency conversion iFlow. The objective was to expose an HTTPS endpoint, call an external currency conversion API, and return the converted amount in a readable format. Along with the happy path, I also wanted to implement proper exception handling, similar to what is expected in real production scenarios.

This blog shares my design approach, iFlow structure, and key learnings from implementing this scenario.

Business Requirement

The requirements I considered while designing this iFlow were:

  • Convert an amount from one currency to another in real time
  • Expose the functionality through a secure HTTPS endpoint
  • Fetch up‑to‑date exchange rates from an external API
  • Return the result in a user‑friendly output
  • Handle errors in a centralized and structured way

This closely mimics real‑world requirements where CPI acts as an integration layer between business applications and external services.

High‑Level Solution Overview

I designed the solution using SAP Cloud Integration following a simple API‑to‑API pattern:

  1. An external system sends a currency conversion request.
  2. CPI receives and processes the request.
  3. CPI calls an external currency conversion API.
  4. CPI formats the response as an HTML output.
  5. In case of any failure, CPI handles errors through an Exception Subprocess and sends notifications.

This approach helped me understand how CPI manages synchronous calls, properties, transformations, and error handling together.

iFlow Design Overview

The iFlow is divided into two main parts:

  • Main Integration Flow – handles the successful processing
  • Global Exception Subprocess – handles errors from any step in the flow

This separation made the design clean and easier to maintain.

I-FLow.

Main Integration Flow – Step‑by‑Step

HTTPS_In

I used an HTTPS sender adapter as the entry point of the iFlow. This allows external systems or tools like Postman to send requests in JSON format.

This step starts the message processing and generates the Message Processing Log (MPL) ID, which is useful for monitoring and troubleshooting.

Why I used it:
Most real‑time integrations in CPI are API‑driven, so HTTPS was the natural choice.

HTTPS sender adapter configuration.

You can choose your own address name. 

JSON2XML_Convert

Since the incoming request is in JSON format, I added a JSON to XML converter. This made it easier to work with the payload in CPI, especially for extracting values using content modifiers or XPath.

Learning:
Converting early helps avoid complications in later steps.

Set_ConvParams (Content Modifier)

In this step, I extracted the source currency, target currency, and amount and stored them as Exchange Properties.

I purposely used properties instead of reading values repeatedly from the payload, as this improves readability and reusability across the flow.

Why I did this:
Using properties keeps later steps independent of payload structure.

Content Modifier (Properties tab)

Call_RateAPI

Here, I used a Request‑Reply pattern to call an external currency conversion API. Since the requirement was to return the converted amount immediately, a synchronous call was necessary.

During testing, I intentionally triggered API errors to verify whether my exception handling was working correctly.

Learning:
Request‑Reply is ideal when immediate responses are required.

 

Build_HTML_Result

After receiving the API response, I used a Groovy script to extract the converted amount and build an HTML‑formatted output.

Instead of returning raw JSON or plain text, I formatted the result as an HTML table containing:

  • Source currency
  • Target currency
  • Source amount
  • Converted amount
  • Timestamp (IST)

Why I did this:
HTML output improves readability for users, testers, and demos.

HTTP_Out

Finally, the formatted HTML response is sent back to the caller. This allows the result to be previewed directly in Postman or a browser.

Use case:
This is very helpful during testing and demonstrations without needing a separate UI.

Exception Subprocess – Centralized Error Handling

While building the flow, I realized that handling only the success scenario was not enough. So I implemented a Global Exception Subprocess to handle failures consistently.

Err_Start

This step is triggered automatically whenever an error occurs in any part of the main flow, such as:

  • API failures
  • Groovy errors
  • Payload or conversion issues

Err_Build_HTML

In this step, I extracted error details such as:

  • MPL ID
  • Error type
  • Error message
  • Timestamp

I formatted these details into an HTML table, similar to the success response. This made the error output structured and easy to understand.

Learning:
Readable error messages save a lot of troubleshooting time.

Mail_Out_Err

I added a Mail adapter to send the formatted error details to a support email ID. This ensures that failures are noticed immediately without checking CPI logs manually.

Real‑world relevance:
This is commonly used in production environments for monitoring.

Err_End

The exception flow ends cleanly after sending the notification, ensuring proper message lifecycle completion.

The above one is for incorrect input payload 

Benefits of This Approach

  • Practical understanding of API‑based integrations in SAP CPI
  • Importance of Exchange Properties for clean design
  • How to implement a reusable Exception Subprocess
  • Benefits of HTML‑formatted responses for both success and errors
  • How CPL logs (MPL ID) help in monitoring and support

Conclusion

This exercise helped me gain hands‑on experience in building a real‑time integration scenario using SAP Cloud Integration. By implementing both success and error handling properly, I was able to design an iFlow that closely resembles a real‑world project.

The same design pattern can be reused for many API‑driven integration scenarios, making it a valuable learning experience.

entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon in a DemoJam

entry.title: Using cXML Envelopes in Integration Advisor
entry.content:
I am happy to announce that we have extended our envelope support to the cXML Type System. Our B2B Library of predefined content now includes the envelope structures for cXML as well. As a result, you can now combine cXML Envelope and cXML Message parts into one Message Implementation Guideline (MIG). Doing so, you can use the envelope fields at other places like in Mapping Guidelines (MAGs). In this blog post I want to give you an overview of the new offering.

cXML Envelopes in Type System Content

The Type System Content in Integration Advisor was extended to contain the cXML Envelopes as well (starting from version 1.2.044). In Message Overview you can simply search for Envelope to find the according structures. 

cXML defines three different envelope structures: the EnvelopeForRequests, the EnvelopeForResponses plus the EnvelopeForMessages. Note as a cXML specialty that the choice of the Envelope depends on the Message Type:

  • Use EnvelopeForRequests for so called cXML Request messages like OrderRequest or InvoiceRequest.
  • Use EnvelopeForResponses for so called cXML Response messages like DataResponse or OrderStatusSetupResponse.
  • Use EnvelopeForMessages for so called cXML Message messages like SupplierChangeMessage or ProductActivityMessage.

By the way, if you navigate to the Message placeholder node within the envelope structures, you will find the fitting message types (shown as allowed code values).

Refer above screenshot for a cXML EnvelopeForRequests structure. Please note that the envelope structure is independent from the specific message(s) used and only shows a placeholder for the message level.

Message Implementation Guidelines with Envelopes 

For your Message Implementation Guideline (MIG) you have now the new option to combine Envelope and Message parts into one merged structure. 

When creating a new MIG for the cXML Type System, you will find the step Envelopes as part of the Creation Wizard. Here you can decide if you want to use an envelope or not and select the specific envelope structure you want to use.

As a result, your MIG starts with the root node cXML and has the envelope parts (see blue box above) plus the specific message parts (see green box above) according to the Message Type you have selected for your MIG. Refer above screenshot for an exemplary cXML OrderRequest MIG which includes the EnvelopeForRequests (with nodes Header and Request).

Envelope Usage in Cloud Integration

Previously, your Integration Flow in Cloud Integration needed an additional flow step to remove the envelope parts from the cXML payload. You don’t need this flow step anymore if you use an envelope inside your cXML MIG.

Additionally, we implemented another small improvement: If your MIG does not include an Envelope, then the Integration Advisor preprocessing (preproc.xslt) automatically removes the envelope part from the payload. This applies to all Type Systems that offer envelopes. In this way, you can even skip this additional flow step for MIGs without envelope.

Further cXML Content Improvements in Integration Advisor

As we have updated our cXML content, we used the chance to add a few more improvements to it (in accordance with the cXML Reference Guide):

  • All date/time fields (based on Simple Type datetime.tz) and all time fields (based on Simple Type time.tz) refer to a new special Date Time Format required by cXML. In both cases, this is the standard ISO 8601 format, but with mandatory time zone offset and Z not being allowed (like 2026-04-01T11:11:11+02:00 for Date/Time or 11:11:11+02:00 for Time).
  • The common xml:lang attribute is now used in its standard form inside your MIG.
  • All fields based on Simple Type UnitOfMeasure come now with a reference to the codelist Rec 20 from UN/CEFACT. 

Conclusion

With this extension we support envelope structures for the cXML Type System in Integration Advisor. You can now include envelope nodes into your Message Implementation Guideline (MIG) definition and as result use these nodes inside your Mapping Guidelines (MAGs). 

Further reading

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integration-advisor-overview-of-components-for-... 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/integration-content-advisor-create-a-customized...

entry.title: Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI Driven Operations
entry.content:
Clean Core for Utilities: The Missing Link Between S/4HANA and AI‑Driven Operations

 

Most of the Utilities organization are entering one of the most disruptive decades the industry has ever seen. Aging assets due to modern technologies, rising customer expectations due to digital world, and the rapid growth of renewables are forcing companies to rethink how they operate and manage their system. Many utilities organizations have already started their S/4HANA programs with the hope that modernization will naturally lead to automation, intelligence, and AI-driven insights.

But here’s the reality most teams discover the hard way:

AI doesn’t scale on a messy foundation. And without a Clean Core, S/4HANA alone won’t get you there.

Across utilities in the U.S., APAC, Europe and the Middle East, the organizations making real progress but having  one thing in common: they aren’t just migrating — they’re rebuilding their architectural backbone from scratch of the system or existing design so AI can deliver business value the way they want it to get modern success.

Why Utilities Are Still Struggling

Many utilities organizations are carrying 15–25 years of technical debt. Their ECC  or R3 and IS‑U systems often include:

  • Custom code buried in user exits, BADI, FQ Event and enhancements both implicit and explicit.
  • Hard‑coded logic only a developer or few team member understand.
  • Manual steps scattered across the meter‑to‑cash cycle.
  • Data spread across GIS, AMI, OMS, CIS and legacy and other third-party tools.

The result is a fragmented landscape where every process behaves differently. And that’s the real blocker. Many utilities think their challenge is adopting AI. But the truth is:

AI cannot operate on inconsistent data or unpredictable processes.

What Clean Core Actually Means         

This important point is most of the people misconception that Clean Core means “no customization.” That’s not realistic for utilities, at least for now — regulatory and market needs are too complex, and greater flexibility is still needed, but future may change for above statement is true for now.

Clean Core is not about eliminating logic. It’s about putting logic in the right place.

A practical Clean Core approach means:

1. Keep SAP S/4HANA Utilities standard

Use it as a stable, upgrade‑safe system of record for metering, billing, finance, and core operations.

2. Move differentiation to SAP BTP

Build workflows, extensions, and apps on BTP — not inside the core.

3. Use events instead of modifications

A meter read posted in SAP S/4HANA Utilities should trigger an event. That event can activate workflows, validations, or AI agents on BTP — without touching billing logic.

Clean Core doesn’t slow innovation. It creates the conditions for innovation to scale safely.

Why Clean Core Is the Missing Link for AI

AI only works well when three things are true:

  • Data is consistent, this must have.
  • Processes are predictable, of course this is something to understandable.
  • System behavior is stable; this is bottleneck as well.

Clean Core enforces all three.

Without it, AI stays stuck in pilots’ phase. With it, AI becomes operational. Utilities that adopt Clean Core unlock outcomes which are:

  • Predictive billing to reduce customer call and dispute resolution.
  • Autonomous collections based on customer behavior. Important for utility industries.
  • DER/EV readiness without breaking billing logic, of course main source of revenue generation.
  • Revenue integrity through real‑time anomaly detection.

This shifts from reactive operations to predictive and autonomous ones and trust me this is future of utilities at least from my prospective with AI Era and digital world we are moving in.

Reference Architecture for an AI‑Ready Utility

This future architecture can be understood in three layers:

1.       Transactional Core (SAP S/4HANA Utilities)
  • System of record for metering, billing, and finance data
  • Standardized and upgrade‑friendly
  • Minimal custom code
2.       Innovation Layer (SAP BTP)

Where differentiation lives. Using:

  • Integration Suite
  • Build Process Automation
  • Event Mesh
  • AI Foundation
  • Joule Agents
  • Customer microservices

This is where utilities build intelligent workflows and automation — without touching the core.

3.       AI‑Driven Operations

The outcome layer:

  • Predictive insights
  • Automated processes
  • AI agents managing meter‑to‑cash
  • Intelligent customer interactions
  • Real‑time decisioning

This isn’t just a tech stack — it’s a new operating model.

A Practical Maturity Path: Utilities don’t need to jump to AI overnight. A realistic path looks like which I feel should be:

  • Level 1: Customized Core (Reactive): Heavy custom code, manual processes, limited visibility.
  • Level 2: Clean Core + BTP (Responsive): Standard core with side‑by‑side innovation.
  • Level 3: Event‑Driven + AI (Autonomous): Real‑time processes with AI‑driven decisioning.

The goal isn’t perfection — it’s steady movement toward a scalable, intelligent architecture.

 

 

How to Get Started

Here is practical first steps:

  1. Assess your landscape, identify custom code that can be retired or moved out of the core.
  2. Adopt standard processes first S/4HANA already solves many historical customizations.
  3. Build side‑by‑side by default innovation belongs on BTP.
  4. Prioritize data quality AI is only as good as the data feeding it.

Conclusion

Clean Core is no longer an architectural preference — it’s a prerequisite for AI‑driven utilities.

The future utility isn’t just delivering power or water or gas. It’s managing data, events, and intelligent processes in real time.

Utilities that recognize this shift early won’t just modernize — they’ll redefine how the industry operates.
